pmos

Context Engineering for AI — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A large language model — the kind of AI behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini — only knows what you put in front of it at the moment you ask. Everything it can see while answering lives in one place called the context window. Context engineering is the discipline of deciding what goes into that window: the instructions, the examples, the retrieved documents, the conversation so far, the memories, and the results of any tools the model used. This primer is the applied map of that discipline — not how a model works inside, but what you feed it. It opens with what context engineering is and how it differs from the older idea of prompt engineering. It explains the context window as the model's working memory, and the surprising finding that a bigger window is not automatically a better one — quality can quietly degrade as the input grows, a problem researchers call "context rot." It then walks the anatomy of a context piece by piece: retrieval-augmented generation (RAG, the technique of looking facts up before answering), prompt structure and instructions, few-shot examples, short- and long-term memory, and the tool results that agents generate. It covers how to keep a long conversation from overflowing through compaction and summarization, how to evaluate and debug a context that misbehaves, and what all of this means for a product manager who owns an AI feature. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out — no machine-learning background assumed. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the practitioner canon — Anthropic, Firecrawl, Elastic, Memgraph, and DigitalOcean for the framing; IBM, Databricks, and Wikipedia for retrieval; Chroma's research lab for the context-rot study; Anthropic's docs and Learn Prompting for prompt structure; OpenAI's cookbook and Amazon Web Services for memory and agents; Product School for the PM lens. This is a fast-moving area — the term itself only became common in 2025 — so several framings here are still being argued over by practitioners. 16 verified sources.

What context engineering is

Start with the one fact that makes everything else make sense: a large language model has no memory of you between requests, and no live connection to your files, your database, or today's news. A large language model (LLM) is the kind of artificial intelligence that predicts text — it is the engine inside chat assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. When you ask it something, it answers using two things only: the patterns it absorbed during training, and the exact block of text you hand it right now. That block of text is the model's entire world for that one answer.

Context engineering is the work of deciding what belongs in that block. Anthropic, the company behind the Claude models, defines it in their engineering guide as "the set of strategies for curating and maintaining the optimal set of tokens (information) during LLM inference, including all the other information that may land there outside of the prompts." In plainer words: it is choosing, shaping, and trimming everything the model sees — not just the question you typed, but the instructions, the reference documents, the conversation history, and anything a tool fetched along the way.

A widely shared working definition, quoted by the developer-tools company Firecrawl, frames it as "the discipline of designing dynamic systems that provide the right information and tools, in the right format, at the right time, to give an LLM everything it needs to accomplish a task." Firecrawl also passes along a blunter version from Shopify's chief executive Tobi Lutke, who called it "the art of providing all the context for the task to be plausibly solvable by the LLM." The word "plausibly" is doing real work there: if the facts the model needs are not in front of it, no amount of clever wording will conjure them.

This is a young idea. The phrase "context engineering" only entered common use in 2025, as teams building on top of LLMs noticed that their hardest problems were not about phrasing prompts but about supplying the right material. Because it is new, the boundaries are still being drawn — practitioners do not fully agree on where prompt engineering ends and context engineering begins, and the vocabulary is shifting month to month. Treat the definitions here as the current consensus, not settled doctrine.

Context engineering versus prompt engineering

The cleanest way to understand context engineering is to set it next to the thing it grew out of. Prompt engineering is the older, narrower craft. Wikipedia defines it as "the process of structuring natural language inputs (known as prompts) to produce specified outputs from a generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) model." It is the art of wording your request well — being specific, giving the model a role, telling it the format you want.

Context engineering is broader. The search company Elastic draws the line crisply: "Prompt engineering focuses on how you communicate with the model. Context engineering focuses on what information the model has access to when it generates responses." One is about phrasing; the other is about supply. A popular way to say it is that prompt engineering is what you do inside the context window, while context engineering is how you decide what fills the window.

The graph-database company Memgraph frames the difference as two different questions. Prompt engineering, they write, "asks: How should the model behave for this task?" while context engineering "asks: What is the model allowed to know and use for this task?" They place prompt engineering "close to the model" — it "shapes language and behaviour" — and context engineering "in the architecture around the model," where it "shapes what the model can see."

Why does the distinction matter in practice? Because, as Memgraph puts it, "Prompt tweaks cannot fix missing or bad context." If your support bot does not have the customer's order history in front of it, rewording the prompt fifty times will not make it answer correctly — the order history simply is not there. This is the central insight of the field: a large share of "the model gave a wrong answer" problems are really "the model never had the right information" problems in disguise.

It is worth flagging that not everyone loves the two-camp framing. Anthropic itself describes context engineering as "the natural progression of prompt engineering" rather than a rival to it — prompt engineering becomes one ingredient inside the larger task. So read "versus" loosely. The two are layers of the same job, not opponents.

The context window as working memory

To engineer something, you need to know its shape and its limit. The context window is the fixed amount of text a model can consider at one time. Everything the model uses to produce an answer — your instructions, the documents you pasted, the back-and-forth so far — has to fit inside this window. Anything outside it might as well not exist.

Text is measured in tokens, the small chunks a model reads — roughly three-quarters of a word each, so a token is a bit shorter than an average word. A context window is sized in tokens. Wikipedia, describing how a model decides which words relate to which, notes that the attention machinery works only "within the scope of the context window" — the window is the boundary of what the model can weigh at once. Early models were tiny by today's standards: Wikipedia records that the small 2019-era GPT-2 model had "a context window of only 1k tokens" — about 750 words, less than two pages.

Modern windows are vastly larger — hundreds of thousands of tokens, sometimes more, enough to hold whole books. That growth is what makes context engineering both possible and necessary. Possible, because you can now fit real reference material alongside the question. Necessary, because a bigger pantry does not cook the meal — someone still has to decide what goes in.

The most useful mental model is to treat the context window as the model's working memory — the mental desk it spreads its papers on while it thinks. A human working at a cluttered desk, with the wrong papers on top, works worse than one with a clean desk and exactly the right page in front of them. The same is true here, and the next section explains why that desk metaphor is more literal than it sounds.

Why bigger isn't always better: context rot

The intuitive assumption — that if the model can hold more, you should give it more — turns out to be wrong, and this is one of the most important findings in the whole field. Anthropic states the principle directly: "context must be treated as a finite resource with diminishing marginal returns." Like a person with limited working memory, a model gets worse, not better, when you flood it.

The evidence comes from a 2025 study by the research lab at Chroma, a database company, which named the effect context rot. The study's blunt finding: "models do not use their context uniformly; instead, their performance grows increasingly unreliable as input length grows." The researchers tested "18 LLMs, including the state-of-the-art GPT-4.1, Claude 4, Gemini 2.5, and Qwen3 models" — a broad, current sample, not a fluke of one system. And they found that "even under these minimal conditions, model performance degrades as input length increases, often in surprising and non-uniform ways."

"Non-uniform" is the key word. The model does not read a long context evenly from top to bottom. Information buried in the middle of a long input is more easily missed than information at the very start or the very end — a pattern practitioners call "lost in the middle." So the same fact can be answered correctly when it sits near the top of a short context and missed when it is sandwiched in the middle of a long one. Anthropic ties this directly to context rot, summarizing the research as showing that "as the number of tokens in the context window increases, the model's ability to accurately recall information from that context decreases."

The practical lesson follows naturally. Firecrawl distills the goal of context engineering to a single sentence: "Find the minimum set of tokens that maximizes the likelihood of your desired outcome." Not the maximum — the minimum that still works. More context is a cost as well as a benefit: it adds noise, it adds expense (you pay per token), and past a point it actively lowers quality. The skill is curation, not accumulation.

The anatomy of a context

If context engineering is deciding what fills the window, it helps to know what the candidate ingredients are. The pieces are remarkably consistent across the practitioner sources. DigitalOcean, a cloud company, lists what it calls the parts of the input the model sees: "the set of information (instructions, examples, retrieved documents, system messages, conversation history, constraints, etc.) that the model sees." Elastic's list overlaps almost exactly: the prompt and instructions, "documents or external data you retrieve through retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)," and "past state, tool calls, or other history."

Pulling the sources together, a typical context is assembled from these layers:

  • System prompt and instructions — the standing rules: who the assistant is, how it should behave, what format to use.
  • The user's request — the actual question or task for this turn.
  • Examples — sample inputs and outputs that show the model the pattern you want (covered later as few-shot examples).
  • Retrieved documents — facts looked up on demand from your own data, via retrieval-augmented generation.
  • Conversation history — the earlier turns of the current exchange.
  • Memory — durable notes carried across sessions, distinct from the live conversation.
  • Tool definitions and tool results — the actions the model can take, and whatever those actions returned.

Two things are worth noticing about this list. First, all of it competes for the same finite window — every retrieved document is space a few-shot example cannot use, which is why curation matters. DigitalOcean's advice is to "curate, filter, and inject only the most useful content" rather than dumping everything in. Second, most of these layers are dynamic — they change from request to request. The system prompt is usually fixed, but the retrieved documents, the history, and the tool results are assembled fresh each time. That assembly is the engineering. The rest of this primer takes the major layers one at a time.

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

The single most important technique for getting facts into a context is retrieval-augmented generation, almost always shortened to RAG. The idea is simple: before the model answers, a separate step searches a body of trusted documents, pulls out the passages most relevant to the question, and pastes them into the context window. The model then answers using those passages instead of relying only on its training.

IBM Research defines RAG as "an AI framework for retrieving facts from an external knowledge base to ground large language models (LLMs) on the most accurate, up-to-date information." The word "ground" matters: grounding means tying the model's answer to real, citable source material rather than letting it improvise. IBM describes the two-step flow plainly — algorithms "search for and retrieve snippets of information relevant to the user's prompt or question," and then "the LLM draws from the augmented prompt" to write its answer. IBM offers a memorable image for the whole thing: it is "the difference between an open-book and a closed-book exam." A model without RAG sits a closed-book exam, answering from memory; with RAG, it answers with the book open in front of it.

Why go to the trouble? Two reasons, and the sources agree on both. The first is freshness. Wikipedia notes that RAG lets "LLMs first refer to a specified set of documents, then respond to user queries," which lets them "use domain-specific and/or updated information that is not available in the training data." A model's training has a cutoff date and knows nothing about your private company data; RAG bridges both gaps. The second reason is accuracy. A hallucination is when a model states something false with full confidence. Wikipedia observes that RAG "helps reduce AI hallucinations, which have caused chatbots to describe policies that don't exist."

Databricks, a data-and-AI platform company, gives a parallel definition — RAG is "a hybrid AI framework that bolsters large language models (LLMs) by combining them with external, up-to-date data sources" — and stresses a practical advantage: it makes outputs "more accurate, current and domain-specific" without the expense of retraining the model. That last point is why RAG became the default pattern for serious products. Retraining a model on your data is slow and costly; retrieving from your data at question time is cheap and updates instantly when the data changes.

One honest caveat: RAG is only as good as what it retrieves and how that retrieved text sits in the window. Pull in the wrong passages, or bury the right ones in the middle of a bloated context, and RAG can mislead as confidently as it helps. Retrieval quality is its own engineering problem, and it is where many RAG systems quietly fail.

Prompt structure and instructions

Retrieval gets the facts in; structure decides whether the model can tell them apart. When a context mixes instructions, reference documents, examples, and the user's question, the model needs help knowing which is which. The most established technique is to mark the sections explicitly.

Anthropic's prompting guide recommends using tags to label the parts: "XML tags help Claude parse complex prompts unambiguously, especially when your prompt mixes instructions, context, examples, and variable inputs." (XML tags are simple labels like <instructions> and <document> that wrap a section so its boundaries are obvious.) The same principle holds whether you use tag-style labels or plain headings — the point is that clearly delineated sections are read more reliably than one undifferentiated wall of text.

Order matters too, especially as contexts get long. Anthropic advises putting bulky reference material first: "Place your long documents and inputs near the top of your prompt, above your query." The question goes last, after the material it draws on. This dovetails with the "lost in the middle" finding from the context-rot section — the start and end of a context are the most reliably attended-to real estate, so the instruction you most need followed should not be buried in the middle.

The instructions themselves should be specific and behavioral. This is where classic prompt engineering lives inside the larger context job: say what you want plainly, give the model a role, name the output format, and prefer telling it what to do over what to avoid. None of this is new — but in a long, RAG-fed context it becomes load-bearing, because the model is now juggling far more material than a one-line prompt ever asked it to.

Few-shot examples

Some things are easier to show than to describe, and models are no exception. A few-shot example is a worked sample — an input paired with the ideal output — that you place in the context so the model can copy the pattern. The name comes from how many examples you give. Learn Prompting defines few-shot prompting as providing "two or more examples, which helps the model recognize patterns and handle more complex tasks," and notes that "with more examples, the model gains a better understanding of the task, leading to improved accuracy and consistency."

The contrast is zero-shot prompting, where you give no examples at all. As Learn Prompting puts it, in the zero-shot case "the model has to rely entirely on its pre-trained knowledge to figure out how to complete the task." Zero-shot is fine for simple, common requests; few-shot earns its keep when the task is unusual, when the output format is fussy, or when consistency across many answers matters.

There is a deeper idea underneath. Wikipedia calls the model's ability to pick up a pattern from examples in the prompt in-context learning, and stresses that it is temporary: "Unlike training and fine-tuning, which produce lasting changes, in-context learning is temporary." The model has not actually learned anything permanent — it is mimicking the pattern for this one answer, then forgetting it. That is why examples have to be re-supplied every time, and why they count against your token budget like everything else.

On quantity and quality, Anthropic's guidance is practical: examples "can dramatically improve accuracy and consistency," and the rule of thumb is to "Include 3–5 examples for best results." Good examples are relevant to the real task and diverse enough to cover the edge cases — a set that is all near-identical teaches the model only the narrow case it shows. As always, examples trade off against everything else in the window, so a few sharp ones beat a dozen redundant ones.

Memory: short-term and long-term

So far the pieces have been about a single request. But assistants and agents run across many turns and many sessions, and they need to remember things. Memory is the layer that carries information forward — and it splits cleanly into two kinds.

OpenAI's developer cookbook describes short-term memory as the recent conversation, managed by keeping only the latest turns: "keep only the last N turns (a 'turn' = one user message and everything until the next user message)." This is the live working context — what was said a moment ago. Long-term memory is different in kind: the cookbook describes it as compressing prior exchanges into structured summaries that persist across session boundaries, so an agent can remember commitments and constraints from days or weeks earlier. Short-term memory is the conversation; long-term memory is the durable note you keep about the user after the conversation ends.

The reason this split exists is the finite window again. You cannot keep every turn of a long-running relationship in the context — it would overflow, and it would rot. So the practical move is to keep recent turns verbatim and distill older ones into summaries. OpenAI's cookbook spells out why disciplined memory pays off: it keeps the agent "anchored to the latest user goal without dragging along stale details," and because "smaller, sharper prompts cut tokens per turn and attention load," it is cheaper and more accurate at once.

One vivid idea from the same source is worth keeping: good summaries act as "clean rooms" that can correct or omit prior mistakes. When you summarize a messy conversation, you get a chance to leave the dead ends and errors behind, so they stop polluting every future turn. Memory, done well, is not just compression — it is cleanup.

Tool results and agent context

The newest and fastest-moving frontier is the AI agent, and agents make context engineering both harder and more important. An agent is an AI system that does not just answer but acts — it can call tools, take steps, and pursue a goal over many turns. Amazon Web Services defines agentic AI as "an autonomous AI system that can act independently to achieve pre-determined goals," working through stages where "the act stage orchestrates several subtasks that the agentic models will then tackle sequentially."

A tool, in this world, is any external capability the model can invoke — a web search, a database query, a calculator, a code run. Amazon Web Services notes that agents can "access administrator-installed plugins" across software systems and lean on "long-term memory systems to ensure that situational and context-dependent tasks remain consistent." The crucial point for context engineering is this: every time an agent uses a tool, the tool's output comes back as more text in the context window. A search returns ten results; a database query returns a hundred rows; a file read returns a whole document. All of it lands in the same finite window.

This is why agents are the place context engineering gets serious. A single chat turn assembles its context once. An agent assembles and reassembles its context dozens of times in one task, each loop adding the latest tool result on top of everything before it. Without active management, that pile grows until it overflows or rots. Anthropic frames context engineering largely through this agent lens — managing "the entire context state (system instructions, tools, Model Context Protocol (MCP), external data, message history, etc)" across a long-running loop. (Model Context Protocol, or MCP, is a standard way for agents to connect to external tools and data — useful to recognize, not essential to this primer.)

The practical discipline is to feed tool results back selectively: keep the parts that matter, drop the raw bulk, and summarize as you go. An agent that pastes every full tool output into its context will rot long before it finishes the job. Deciding what of each result is worth keeping is context engineering in its most demanding form.

Context compaction and summarization

When a context threatens to overflow — in a long chat or a long-running agent — you need a way to shrink it without losing the thread. The umbrella term is compaction, and the main tool is summarization: replace a long stretch of earlier conversation with a short summary that preserves what matters.

There are two timing strategies, and the distinction is practical. The reactive approach waits until the context is nearly full, then pauses to summarize the older material before continuing. The simplest reactive method is a sliding window — drop the oldest turns as new ones arrive — but that risks discarding something important from early on. The proactive approach summarizes in the background as the conversation goes, so a compact summary is ready before the window fills and no pause is needed. OpenAI's cookbook captures the goal of any compaction scheme: keep the agent "anchored to the latest user goal without dragging along stale details."

Compaction is where the "clean room" idea from the memory section becomes a working habit. A good summary is not a neutral transcript — it is an edited one. It keeps the decisions, the constraints, and the current goal, and it quietly drops the false starts, the corrected mistakes, and the noise. Done well, a conversation that gets compacted several times can stay sharper than one that hoards every word, because each compaction is a chance to clear the rot.

This is an area still being actively figured out — how aggressively to compact, what to keep, when to summarize, how to avoid summarizing away a detail that turns out to matter later. Practitioners do not agree on the answers, and the tooling is changing quickly. Treat compaction as a live research area rather than a solved one.

Evaluating and debugging a context

Because context is assembled dynamically, it can fail in ways that are invisible until you look. The most useful framing of these failures comes from Firecrawl, which catalogs four distinct ways "Context fails in four distinct ways - Poisoning, distraction, confusion, and clash each require different fixes." Poisoning is when a wrong fact enters the context and contaminates everything after it. Distraction is when irrelevant material pulls the model off-task. Confusion is when too much similar material blurs together. Clash is when two pieces of context contradict each other. Each has a different remedy, so naming the failure is the first step to fixing it.

Knowing the failure modes only helps if you can detect them, and the answer is measurement. Firecrawl's guidance for builders is to track task success rates across different context lengths, hallucination rates, and how fresh the retrieved information is — concrete numbers that reveal when a context starts to rot or drift. Without measurement, context problems hide: the model produces a confident, fluent, wrong answer, and nothing flags it.

This is also where the discipline connects to ordinary product practice. Product School urges AI teams to "Run rigorous AI evals" and to "Set dual success metrics" that track "both product outcomes ... and model evaluation metrics (precision, recall, latency)." An eval (short for evaluation) is a repeatable test of model behavior against known-good answers. For a context-engineered system, evals are how you tell whether a change to what you put in the window actually made answers better or just different.

What this means for product managers

For a product manager who owns an AI feature, context engineering reframes where the work is. The instinct is to treat a disappointing AI feature as a model problem — wait for a better model, or spend weeks rewording the prompt. The lesson of this field is that a large share of failures are context problems: the model never had the information it needed, or it had too much, or the wrong piece sat in the wrong place. Memgraph's line is the one to internalize — "Prompt tweaks cannot fix missing or bad context." Before asking for a smarter model, ask what was actually in the window.

This raises the value of unglamorous work. Product School stresses that data fluency is now core to the job: AI product managers must understand "the data your product and business generate, and how AI might use it," including "the importance of data quality." The quality of your retrieved documents, the cleanliness of your knowledge base, and the freshness of your data are now product features — they decide whether RAG grounds the model or misleads it. The boring data work is the AI work.

It also argues for restraint, in two directions. First, on whether to use AI at all: Product School's discipline check is direct — "If simple logic or deterministic rules solve the issue cleanly, use them." Not every problem needs a model. Second, on how much to feed it: the context-rot research is a standing argument against the "stuff everything in the giant window" reflex. Firecrawl's minimum-tokens principle — "Find the minimum set of tokens that maximizes the likelihood of your desired outcome" — is as much a product principle as an engineering one. More is not the goal; right is.

Finally, this is a fast-moving area, and a PM should hold its specifics loosely. The vocabulary, the tooling, and even the boundary between prompt and context engineering are all shifting. What is stable is the underlying truth: the model sees only what you put in front of it, that space is finite, and deciding what goes there is now a first-class part of building an AI product.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • How large language models work — the internals this primer deliberately skips: tokens, attention, and why a model predicts text. Context engineering is what you feed the model; that topic is what happens inside it.
  • Prompt engineering — the narrower craft of wording a single request well, which lives as one layer inside context engineering.
  • Retrieval-augmented generation in depth — embeddings, vector databases, chunking, and re-ranking: the machinery that decides which documents RAG actually retrieves.
  • AI agents — autonomous systems that loop, call tools, and act, where context management becomes the central engineering challenge.
  • AI evaluation — how to measure model and context quality with repeatable tests, the discipline that tells you whether a context change helped.
  • Building AI products — the broader product-management practice of shipping, measuring, and governing AI features responsibly.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_context-engineering-for-ai.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
